title: Quotients of M-convex sets and M-convex functions
overall_summary: The paper unifies quotient notions for matroids, polymatroids, valuated matroids, submodular functions, M-convex sets, and M-convex functions using discrete convex analysis. Its main finite theorem gives ten equivalent characterizations of a quotient P ↠ Q of M-convex sets, including submodular inequalities, top and bottom layers of an M♮-convex set, deletion-contraction from a larger M-convex set, asymmetric exchange, induction through linking sets, Green preorder in a linking-set monoid, compatible matroid lifts, and flag polytope compression. It then develops a partial hierarchy for quotients of M-convex functions, with special consequences for valuated matroids via minimizers and flag-matroid subdivisions. For the original problem, the most relevant content is the translation of containment/quotient data for tropical linear spaces into valuated matroid quotient criteria, especially the minimizer criterion, induction/linking-set formalism, and top-bottom generalized polymatroid realization, any of which could be used either to construct a candidate order-reversing operation or to produce obstructions.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.6
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let E be a finite set and write x(A)=sum_{i∈A} x_i for x∈ℤ^E. A nonempty bounded set P⊆ℤ^E is M-convex if for all x,y∈P and all i with x_i>y_i there is j with x_j<y_j such that x-e_i+e_j and y+e_i-e_j both lie in P. A normalized integral submodular function is a map p:2^E→ℤ with p(∅)=0 and p(A∪B)+p(A∩B)≤p(A)+p(B) for all A,B⊆E. Its base polyhedron is B(p)={x∈ℝ^E: x(A)≤p(A) for all A⊆E and x(E)=p(E)}. The theorem gives a one-to-one correspondence among bounded M-convex sets P⊆ℤ^E, integral submodular functions p:2^E→ℤ, and integral generalized permutohedra: if p is submodular then B(p)∩ℤ^E is M-convex; if P is M-convex then p(A)=max{x(A):x∈P} is submodular and P=B(p)∩ℤ^E; and P is M-convex iff P=conv(P)∩ℤ^E and conv(P) is an integral generalized permutohedron, i.e. a polytope whose edge directions are among e_i-e_j and whose vertices are integral.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites Murota for this standard equivalence. The direction from submodular functions uses Edmonds base polyhedra: normalized integral submodular inequalities define an integral polyhedron lying in the hyperplane x(E)=p(E), and its edge directions are e_i-e_j; the integer points satisfy the M-convex exchange axiom. Conversely, for an M-convex set P, defining p(A) by maximizing x(A) over P records all supporting inequalities of conv(P); the exchange axiom forces p to be submodular, and the resulting base polyhedron has exactly P as its lattice points. The generalized permutohedron formulation follows because base polyhedra of submodular functions are precisely generalized permutohedra, and integrality of the vertices is equivalent to recovering the lattice points as an M-convex set.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.10
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let P,Q⊆ℤ^E be bounded M-convex sets with associated normalized integral submodular functions p,q. Say p and q are compliant, or P ↠ Q, if q(Y)-q(X)≤p(Y)-p(X) for every pair X⊆Y⊆E. For each permutation σ of E, let x_σ be the greedy vertex of B(p) and y_σ the greedy vertex of B(q), where for S_k={σ(1),...,σ(k)} the coordinate of x_σ at σ(k) is p(S_k)-p(S_{k-1}), and similarly for y_σ. Then P ↠ Q if and only if x_σ≥y_σ coordinatewise for every σ∈Sym(E).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For a fixed permutation σ, the greedy-coordinate formula expresses each coordinate of x_σ and y_σ as a rank increment along the chain ∅=S_0⊂S_1⊂...⊂S_n=E. If p and q are compliant, applying q(S_k)-q(S_{k-1})≤p(S_k)-p(S_{k-1}) gives y_σ(σ(k))≤x_σ(σ(k)) for every k. Conversely, given X⊆Y, choose a permutation σ whose first |X| entries are X and whose first |Y| entries are Y. Summing the coordinate inequalities x_σ≥y_σ over the block Y∖X gives q(Y)-q(X)≤p(Y)-p(X).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.18 and Lemma 2.17
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a submodular function p:2^E→ℤ, define its dual supermodular function p# by p#(A)=p(E)-p(E∖A). Given a submodular p and a supermodular q satisfying p(A)-q(B)≥p(A∖B)-q(B∖A) for all A,B⊆E, the generalized polymatroid G(p,q) is {x∈ℝ^E:q(A)≤x(A)≤p(A) for all A⊆E}. A bounded set R⊆ℤ^E is M♮-convex if and only if R=G(p,q#)∩ℤ^E for submodular functions p,q for which G(p,q#) is a generalized polymatroid; equivalently, R is the coordinate projection of a bounded M-convex set on a larger ground set. If G(p,q#) is a generalized polymatroid, then its top layer is B(p) and its bottom layer is B(q), so G(p,q#)∩ℤ^E has top layer B(p)∩ℤ^E and bottom layer B(q)∩ℤ^E.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper recalls the standard discrete-convex characterization: coordinate projections of M-convex sets satisfy the M♮ exchange axiom, and every M♮-convex set can be obtained from an M-convex set by adding one coordinate and projecting. Polyhedrally, such projections are exactly generalized polymatroids, i.e. intersections of a submodular polyhedron B(p)+ℝ^E_{≤0} and a supermodular polyhedron B(q)+ℝ^E_{≥0}. The compatibility inequality guarantees that the top base polyhedron B(p) and bottom base polyhedron B(q) are not cut away by the intersection. Hence maximizing x(E) in G(p,q#) gives B(p), while minimizing x(E) gives B(q).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.29 with Propositions 2.31 and 2.32
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let p,q:2^E→ℤ be normalized integral submodular functions. Then p and q are compliant, i.e. q(Y)-q(X)≤p(Y)-p(X) for all X⊆Y⊆E, if and only if G(p,q#) is a generalized polymatroid. Consequently, if P=B(p)∩ℤ^E and Q=B(q)∩ℤ^E, then P ↠ Q if and only if there exists an M♮-convex set R⊆ℤ^E with P=R↑ and Q=R↓; in that case R is explicitly G(p,q#)∩ℤ^E. Conversely, if P and Q are the top and bottom layers of an M♮-convex set, then their associated submodular functions are compliant.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The core cited result of Fujishige and Hirai identifies the generalized-polymatroid compatibility inequalities for G(p,q#) with the strong-map/compliance inequalities for p and q. Once G(p,q#) is known to be a generalized polymatroid, Theorem 2.18 implies that its lattice points form an M♮-convex set, and Lemma 2.17 identifies the top and bottom layers as B(p)∩ℤ^E and B(q)∩ℤ^E. Conversely, any M♮-convex set has a generalized-polymatroid representation G(f,g#); its top and bottom layers determine f and g uniquely by the M-convex/submodular correspondence, and the generalized-polymatroid condition gives compliance.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let P,Q⊆ℤ^E be bounded M-convex sets, and let p,q:2^E→ℤ be their associated normalized submodular functions. The following ten conditions are equivalent, and define Q as a quotient of P, denoted P ↠ Q. (1) For every X⊆Y⊆E, q(Y)-q(X)≤p(Y)-p(X). (2) For every permutation σ of E, the greedy vertices x_σ∈P and y_σ∈Q satisfy x_σ≥y_σ coordinatewise. (3) For every X⊆E, the submodular polyhedron S(q/X) is contained in S(p/X), where p/X(A)=p(A∪X)-p(X). (4) There is an M♮-convex set R⊆ℤ^E such that P is the top layer R↑ and Q is the bottom layer R↓; explicitly R=G(p,q#)∩ℤ^E. (5) There is an M-convex set R⊆ℤ^{E⊔X} such that P=R∖X is a deletion and Q=R/X is a contraction; the added set X can be chosen to be a singleton. (6) For all x∈Q, y∈P, and i∈supp+(x-y), there exists j∈supp−(x-y) such that x-e_i+e_j∈Q and y+e_i-e_j∈P. (7) There exists a linking set Γ⊆ℤ^E×ℤ^{Ē}, meaning an M-convex set viewed from E to Ē, and an M-convex set W⊆ℤ^{Ē}, such that P is the top layer of the left projection π_E(Γ) and Q is the induction ind_Γ(W)={x∈ℤ^E: there is w∈W with (x,-w)∈Γ}. (8) There are linking sets Γ,Δ⊆ℤ^E×ℤ^E such that P and Q are top layers of their left projections and Γ⪯_RΔ in Green's right preorder on the monoid of linking sets, i.e. Γ∗X=Δ for some linking set X. (9) Any compatible matroid lifts of P and Q form a matroid quotient. (10) The Minkowski sum P+Q is a flag M-convex set of type determined by the two ranks rk(Q)+ℓ and rk(P)+ℓ for some integer ℓ and some surjection of ground sets; equivalently it is an integral translate of a projection of a generalized permutohedron whose vertices lie among those of the corresponding sum of two hypersimplices.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The equivalence (1)⇔(2) is Theorem 2.10, using greedy vertices along chains. For (1)⇔(3), expand the contraction formula: S(q/X)⊆S(p/X) is exactly q(A∪X)-q(X)≤p(A∪X)-p(X) for all A⊆E∖X. For (1)⇔(4), use Theorem 2.29 to identify compliance with G(p,q#) being a generalized polymatroid, then use the M♮-convex/generalized-polymatroid correspondence and top-bottom layer lemma. For (4)⇔(5), use that every M♮-convex set is a coordinate projection of an M-convex set, and deletion and contraction are the top and bottom layers of such a projection; one extra coordinate suffices. Condition (4) implies (6) by applying an M♮ exchange axiom to a top-layer point and a bottom-layer point. The implication (6)⇒(1) is proved in two steps: first Lemma 2.35 shows every single-element increment satisfies q(X)-q(X∖i)≤p(X)-p(X∖i), by repeatedly applying the asymmetric exchange property to points x∈Q maximizing q(X) and y∈P maximizing p(X∖i); then induction over |Y∖X| yields the full compliance inequality. The equivalence with (7) is proved by lifting the M♮ set in (4) to one extra coordinate and selecting its bottom layer by induction through a singleton M-convex set; conversely, applying M-convex exchange inside Γ shows the asymmetric exchange property, because an exchange into the auxiliary side would contradict top-layer maximality. The equivalence with (8) uses the product of linking sets: if Γ∗X=Δ then the left set of Δ is the induction through Γ of the left set of X, so (7) applies; conversely, realize the W in (7) as the left set of a linking set and set Δ=Γ∗X. The equivalence with (9) is obtained by constructing box lifts to matroids and using induction to show quotient preservation, while reflection follows because the coordinate-summing projection preserves top and bottom layers. The equivalence with (10) combines compatible matroid lifts with the classical theorem that a pair of matroids is a quotient exactly when the vertices of its Minkowski sum lie in the corresponding flag-matroid polytope.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.26 and Proposition 2.28
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let P⊆ℤ^E be an M-convex set with submodular function p, and write E=V⊔U. For k in the interval min_{x∈P}x(V)≤k≤max_{x∈P}x(V), the basic k-th minor of P with respect to U is P^U_k={x∈ℤ^V: there exists y∈ℤ^U with (x,y)∈P and x(V)=k}=π_V(P)∩H_k. The deletion P∖U is the top such minor, and the contraction P/U is the bottom such minor. Their submodular functions are p∖U(A)=p(A) and p/U(A)=p(A∪U)-p(U) for A⊆V. The submodular function of P^U_k is p^U_k(A)=min(p(A), k+p(A∪U)-p(E∖A)) for A⊆V. If ℓ<k are both in the allowed range, then P^U_k ↠ P^U_ℓ.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Project P to V. The projection π_V(P) is M♮-convex and therefore equals G(f,g#)∩ℤ^V for submodular f,g. Maximizing x(A) over the projection gives f(A)=p(A), while minimizing x(A) gives g#(A)=p(E)-p(E∖A); rewriting g from g# yields p/U(A)=p(A∪U)-p(U). Intersecting the generalized polymatroid π_V(P) with the single layer H_k is intersection with a plank, and the formula for p^U_k follows from the standard plank-intersection formula p^U_k(A)=min(f(A),k-g#(V∖A)). Finally, π_V(P)∩K(ℓ,k), where K(ℓ,k) is the plank ℓ≤x(V)≤k, is M♮-convex; its top and bottom layers are P^U_k and P^U_ℓ, so Theorem 1.2 condition (4) gives P^U_k ↠ P^U_ℓ.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.23
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let P⊆ℤ^E be an M-convex set with submodular function p and rank rk(P). For k>0, the k-th truncation P^tr_k is the layer (P+ℤ^E_{≤0})∩H_{rk(P)-k}. Then P^tr_k is a quotient of P. Moreover, if P ↠ Q and rk(P)-rk(Q)=k, then Q⊆P^tr_k. Thus truncation is the maximal or generic quotient of P of a fixed rank drop.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The submodular function of the k-th truncation agrees with p on all proper subsets A⊊E and has value p(E)-k on E. Compliance is immediate: for X⊆Y⊊E the differences are unchanged, and when Y=E the right side is reduced by k, still giving a quotient in the direction P ↠ P^tr_k. For any quotient P ↠ Q of rank drop k with submodular function q, compliance applied to ∅⊆A gives q(A)≤p(A)=p^tr_k(A) for A⊊E, while q(E)=p(E)-k=p^tr_k(E). Therefore every lattice point of Q satisfies the defining inequalities of P^tr_k, so Q⊆P^tr_k.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemmas 2.38, 2.39, 2.40, and 2.41
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Quotients of M-convex sets are transitive: if P ↠ Q and Q ↠ R, then P ↠ R. A flag of M-convex sets is a sequence (P_0,...,P_k) with P_i ↠ P_{i-1} for all i; it is consecutive if rk(P_i)=rk(P_0)+i. The layers of any M♮-convex set form a consecutive flag of M-convex sets. Conversely, if (P_0,...,P_k) is a consecutive flag and p_0,p_k are the submodular functions of the endpoints, then the layers of G(p_k,p_0#)∩ℤ^E form an M♮-convex flag containing every P_i. Every nonconsecutive flag can be completed to a consecutive flag by inserting intermediate layers.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Transitivity follows by chaining the compliance inequalities r(Y)-r(X)≤q(Y)-q(X)≤p(Y)-p(X). For an M♮-convex set R, any two layers are the top and bottom layers of R intersected with a plank, and plank intersections preserve M♮-convexity; hence the layers form quotients. For embedding a consecutive flag in an M♮-convex flag, let p_i be the submodular function of P_i and set R=G(p_k,p_0#)∩ℤ^E. If x∈P_i, then P_k ↠ P_i gives x(A)≤p_k(A), and P_i ↠ P_0 gives p_0#(A)≤x(A); hence x∈R. Completion of a nonconsecutive flag is done quotient by quotient: for each adjacent pair P_i ↠ P_{i-1}, insert the intermediate layers of the M♮-convex set G(p_i,p_{i-1}#)∩ℤ^E.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.12
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Γ⊆ℤ^V×ℤ^U be a linking set, i.e. an M-convex set viewed as a relation from V to U. If P,Q⊆ℤ^U are M-convex sets with P ↠ Q and the inductions ind_Γ(P) and ind_Γ(Q) are nonempty, then ind_Γ(P) ↠ ind_Γ(Q), where ind_Γ(P)={x∈ℤ^V: there exists y∈P with (x,-y)∈Γ}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Choose an M♮-convex set R⊆ℤ^U with top layer P and bottom layer Q. Induction is built from Minkowski sum and restriction, so ind_Γ(R) is M♮-convex. Nonemptiness ensures the top layer of ind_Γ(R) actually comes from inducing the top layer P: the rank of a point (x,-y)∈Γ with y∈R is x(V)=rk(Γ)+y(U), so maximizing x(V) maximizes y(U), hence y∈P. Similarly the bottom layer comes from y∈Q. Thus ind_Γ(P) and ind_Γ(Q) are the top and bottom layers of ind_Γ(R), so they form a quotient.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Propositions 3.14 and 3.15
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For bounded M-convex sets P,Q⊆ℤ^E, P ↠ Q if and only if there exist a linking set Γ⊆ℤ^E×ℤ^{Ē} and an M-convex set W⊆ℤ^{Ē} such that P is the top layer of the left set π_E(Γ) and Q=ind_Γ(W). Here the auxiliary ground set Ē may be chosen to be a singleton in the construction from a top-bottom M♮-convex realization.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If P ↠ Q, take an M♮-convex set R with top layer P and bottom layer Q. Lift R to an M-convex set Γ on E plus one auxiliary coordinate recording the rank distance from the top layer, with the sign chosen according to the induction convention (x,-w)∈Γ. Taking W to be the singleton equal to the rank drop selects precisely the bottom layer, so ind_Γ(W)=Q, while the top layer of π_E(Γ) is P. Conversely, suppose P=π_E(Γ)↑ and Q=ind_Γ(W). Given x∈Q, y∈P, and i∈supp+(x-y), choose w∈W with (x,-w)∈Γ and choose an auxiliary z with (y,z)∈Γ. M-convex exchange in Γ gives a coordinate j allowing simultaneous exchange. If j were auxiliary, the E-coordinate sum of y would increase, contradicting that y lies in the top layer of π_E(Γ). Therefore j∈E and the asymmetric exchange condition of Theorem 1.2 holds, so P ↠ Q.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definition 3.21 and Proposition 3.24
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a finite set V, let M_V be the monoid of possibly unbounded linking sets Γ⊆ℤ^V×ℤ^V, with product Γ∗Δ={(x,-z): there exists y∈ℤ^V with (x,-y)∈Γ and (y,-z)∈Δ} and identity I_V={(x,-x):x∈ℤ^V}. Green's right preorder is Γ⪯_RΔ if Γ∗X=Δ for some X∈M_V. If Γ⪯_RΔ, then the top layer of the left set of Γ is a quotient of the top layer of the left set of Δ: π_L(Γ)↑ ↠ π_L(Δ)↑. Conversely, for every quotient P ↠ Q of M-convex sets in ℤ^V, there are Γ,Δ∈M_V with P=π_L(Γ)↑, Q=π_L(Δ)↑, and Γ⪯_RΔ.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If Γ∗X=Δ, then the left set of Δ is the induction of the left set of X through Γ: π_L(Δ)=ind_Γ(π_L(X)). Taking the appropriate layer of π_L(X) whose induced rank gives the top layer of π_L(Δ), Proposition 3.14 yields π_L(Γ)↑ ↠ π_L(Δ)↑. Conversely, use the induction characterization of a quotient P ↠ Q: choose Γ and W with P=π_L(Γ)↑ and Q=ind_Γ(W). Realize W as the left set of a linking set X, enlarge ground sets if needed, and set Δ=Γ∗X. Then Γ⪯_RΔ and Q is the top layer of π_L(Δ).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.22
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For |V|>2, the monoid M_V of linking sets is not regular: there exists Γ∈M_V for which no pseudoinverse Γ^{-1} satisfies Γ∗Γ^{-1}∗Γ=Γ.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof uses the partial product of individual pairs (x,-y)∗(z,-w), defined only when y=z. For V containing {1,2,3}, take Γ={ (e_i,-e_j): i,j∈{1,2,3}, i≠j }∪{0}. If a pseudoinverse existed, then for any (e_i,-e_j)∈Γ one would need some (e_k,-e_l) in the pseudoinverse with k≠i and l≠j so that (e_i,-e_k)∗(e_k,-e_l)∗(e_l,-e_j)=(e_i,-e_j). Choosing a∈{1,2,3}∖{k,l} would then force (e_a,-e_a)∈Γ, contradicting the definition of Γ. This structural fact may matter when trying to obtain involutions from the linking-set monoid: the monoid has Green preorders but not pervasive inverses.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Constructions 4.2, 4.4, and 4.6
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Every bounded M-convex set P⊆ℤ^U contained in a box Ω can be lifted through a coordinate-summing map to an M-convex set inside a prescribed box Ψ. Given a surjection ϕ:V→U, the map π_ϕ:ℤ^V→ℤ^U is defined by (π_ϕ(y))_i=sum_{j∈ϕ^{-1}(i)} y_j. If Ω=π_ϕ(Ψ)+v for some v∈ℤ^U, the associated linking set Γ_{ϕ,v}(Ψ) consists of pairs (y,-x) with y∈Ψ and x=π_ϕ(y)+v∈Ω. The box lift of P is L^Ψ_{ϕ,v}(P)=ind_{Γ_{ϕ,v}(Ψ)}(P), an M-convex subset of Ψ with π_ϕ(L^Ψ_{ϕ,v}(P))+v=P. By choosing Ψ=[0,1]^V after splitting each coordinate interval into unit intervals, any bounded M-convex set has a matroid lift. More generally, choosing Ψ⊆[0,k]^V gives a k-polymatroid lift.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The key observation is that the relation x=π_ϕ(y)+v defines an unbounded M-convex set in ℤ^V×ℤ^U, and intersecting it with a box Ψ×(-Ω) preserves M-convexity. Inducing P through this linking set selects exactly those y in Ψ whose projected and translated image lies in P. For matroid lifts, each coordinate interval [ω_i,ω_i] is decomposed as a Minkowski sum of unit intervals [0,1], producing a ground-set fiber V_i and a surjection ϕ. The resulting lift lies in {0,1}^V, hence is the set of bases of a matroid. For k-polymatroid lifts, decompose each coordinate interval into intervals [0,k] and one remainder interval.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Propositions 4.7 and 4.10
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let ϕ:V→U be a surjection and v∈ℤ^U. If M,N⊆ℤ^V are M-convex sets with M ↠ N and P=π_ϕ(M)+v, Q=π_ϕ(N)+v, then P ↠ Q. Conversely, if P,Q⊆ℤ^U satisfy P ↠ Q, then any compatible matroid lifts M,N⊆ℤ^V of P and Q form a matroid quotient M ↠ N. Compatibility means that M and N are produced from the same box-lift data, i.e. the same surjection ϕ, translation v, and box Ψ, so that M=L^Ψ_{ϕ,v}(P) and N=L^Ψ_{ϕ,v}(Q).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For reflection from M,N to P,Q, choose an M♮-convex set R with top layer M and bottom layer N. The coordinate-summing projection π_ϕ and translation by v preserve the order of layers by total coordinate sum, so π_ϕ(R)+v is M♮-convex with top P and bottom Q. For preservation under compatible lifts, view the common box lift as induction through a fixed linking set Γ_{ϕ,v}(Ψ). Since P ↠ Q, Lemma 3.12 gives L^Ψ_{ϕ,v}(P) ↠ L^Ψ_{ϕ,v}(Q). If Ψ=[0,1]^V, these lifts are matroids, so the quotient is a classical matroid quotient.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4.11
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let P⊆ℤ^U be an M-convex set, and let P^tr_k be its k-th truncation. If M,N⊆ℤ^V are compatible matroid lifts of P and P^tr_k, respectively, then N equals the matroid-theoretic k-th truncation of M, namely N=M^tr_k∩ℤ^V_{≥0}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Because P ↠ P^tr_k, compatible lifts give M ↠ N, so N is contained in the M-convex truncation M^tr_k and, being a matroid, in the nonnegative orthant. Conversely, if x∈M^tr_k∩ℤ^V_{≥0}, then x≤y for some y∈M with rank lowered by k. Applying π_ϕ and the common translation v gives π_ϕ(x)+v≤π_ϕ(y)+v∈P, hence π_ϕ(x)+v lies in P^tr_k. By the definition of the common box lift, x∈N.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.12 and Proposition 4.14
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For matroids M,N on the same finite set V, viewed as M-convex subsets of {0,1}^V, the classical flag-matroid polytope criterion says that M ↠ N if and only if the vertices of M+N form a subset of the vertices of the Minkowski sum of hypersimplices Δ(rk(M),V)+Δ(rk(N),V). More generally, an M-convex set R⊆ℤ^U is a Minkowski sum P+Q of two M-convex sets with P ↠ Q if and only if R is a flag M-convex set of the corresponding two-rank type: after an integral translation, R is the image under a coordinate-summing projection π_ϕ of a generalized permutohedron whose vertices are among those of a sum of two hypersimplices with ranks equal to compatible matroid-lift ranks of P and Q.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Use compatible matroid lifts M,N of P,Q. By Propositions 4.7 and 4.10, P ↠ Q is equivalent to M ↠ N for every compatible lift. The matroid criterion of Borovik-Gelfand-White identifies M ↠ N with the vertex condition for M+N inside the sum of two hypersimplices. Projection commutes with Minkowski sum, and the translations in the lift only translate the final M-convex set. Thus P+Q is precisely a projected flag-matroid polytope, which is the definition of a flag M-convex set of the appropriate type.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 5.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let f:ℤ^E→ℝ∪{∞} have nonempty bounded effective domain dom(f)={x:f(x)<∞}. For u∈(ℝ^E)^*, define the minimizer set f^u={x∈ℤ^E: f(x)-⟨u,x⟩≤f(y)-⟨u,y⟩ for all y∈dom(f)}. Then f is an M-convex function if and only if every f^u is an M-convex set. Similarly, f is an M♮-convex function if and only if every f^u is an M♮-convex set.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites Murota. One direction is obtained by applying the exchange axiom for f to minimizers of the linear perturbation f-⟨u,·⟩; the exchange inequality forces the exchanged points to be minimizers as well. Conversely, if an exchange inequality for f failed, a separating linear functional u can be chosen so that the offending points become minimizers while all exchange candidates are more expensive, producing a minimizer set that violates the corresponding M-convex or M♮-convex exchange axiom.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let f,g:ℤ^E→ℝ∪{∞} be M-convex functions with rk(g)<rk(f). Consider four conditions. (A) There exists an M♮-convex function h:ℤ^E→ℝ∪{∞} whose top layer is f and whose bottom layer is g. (B) There exists a linking function γ:ℤ^E×ℤ^{Ē}→ℝ∪{∞}, meaning an M-convex function on the product lattice, and an M-convex function r:ℤ^{Ē}→ℝ∪{∞}, such that f is the top layer of the left function π_E(γ) and g=ind_γ(r), where ind_γ(r)(x)=inf_y(γ(x,-y)+r(y)). (C) For every x∈dom(f), y∈dom(g), and i∈supp+(y-x), there exists j∈supp−(y-x) such that f(x)+g(y)≥f(x+e_i-e_j)+g(y-e_i+e_j). (D) For every u∈(ℝ^E)^*, the minimizer sets f^u and g^u form a quotient of M-convex sets, f^u ↠ g^u. Then (A) implies (B), (B) implies (C), and (C) implies (D). If rk(f)=rk(g)+1, all four conditions are equivalent.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For (A)⇒(B), the proof uses a one-coordinate lift of h as a linking function γ and a singleton M-convex function r supported at the rank difference, with the auxiliary sign chosen so that induction selects the bottom layer; the left function of γ has top layer f. For (B)⇒(C), choose z,w with f(x)=γ(x,w) and g(y)=γ(y,-z)+r(z). Apply the M-convex exchange inequality to the two points (x,w) and (y,-z) of dom(γ). If the exchanged coordinate j lies in E, the desired inequality follows after adding r(z) and taking the definitions of f and g. If j lies in the auxiliary set, then x would move to an E-coordinate sum larger than the top layer of π_E(γ), impossible; hence j must lie in E. For (C)⇒(D), take x∈f^u and y∈g^u. The exchange inequality in (C), after subtracting the same linear terms ⟨u,x⟩ and ⟨u,y⟩ on both sides, shows that the exchanged points are also minimizers; hence f^u and g^u satisfy the asymmetric exchange criterion of Theorem 1.2. For the elementary case rk(f)=rk(g)+1, assume (D) and define h to equal f on dom(f), g on dom(g), and ∞ elsewhere. For every u, the minimizer h^u is either f^u, or g^u, or f^u∪g^u; because f^u ↠ g^u and the ranks differ by one, this union is M♮-convex. The minimizer characterization Theorem 5.2 then gives that h is M♮-convex, establishing (A).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 5.6
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For M-convex functions f,g,h:ℤ^E→ℝ∪{∞} with rk(h)<rk(g)<rk(f), define f ↠_min g to mean that f^u ↠ g^u for every linear functional u. If f ↠_min g and g ↠_min h, then f ↠_min h.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For each u, the minimizer sets are M-convex and satisfy f^u ↠ g^u and g^u ↠ h^u. Quotients of M-convex sets are transitive by Lemma 2.38, so f^u ↠ h^u for every u. This is exactly f ↠_min h.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Remark 5.7
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the special case of valuated matroids, conditions (C) and (D) of Theorem 1.3 are equivalent. Here valuated matroids are M-convex functions whose domains lie in the unit hypercube {0,1}^E.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper invokes Brandt-Eur-Zhang. If f^u ↠ g^u for all u, then by the flag-matroid polytope criterion the Minkowski sum f^u+g^u is the set of lattice points of a flag matroid polytope for every u. Brandt-Eur-Zhang's theorem identifies this subdivision-by-minimizers condition with the valuated quotient exchange condition f ↠_Ex g, which is condition (C). The implication (C)⇒(D) is already part of Theorem 1.3.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let f_0,f_1,...,f_k:ℤ^E→ℝ∪{∞} be M-convex functions with rk(f_i)=rk(f_0)+i and f_i ↠_Ex f_{i-1} for each i=1,...,k, where ↠_Ex denotes the exchange condition (C) of Theorem 1.3. Assume P=⋃_{i=0}^k dom(f_i) is an M♮-convex set. Then there exist constants c_0,...,c_k∈ℝ such that h(x)=inf_i(f_i(x)-c_i) is an M♮-convex function whose layers are f_i-c_i. The paper gives an explicit recursive choice: take c_0=c_1=0, and for m≥2 choose c_m as the minimum of f_m(x)-f_{m-1}(x-e_j)+c_{m-1}-f_{l+1}(y+e_j)+c_{l+1}+f_l(y)-c_l over y∈dom(f_l), 0≤l≤m-2, x∈dom(f_m), and j∈supp−(y-x).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is by induction on k. The case k=1 is the elementary equivalence in Theorem 1.3. Assume h_k=inf_{i≤k}(f_i-c_i) is already M♮-convex. Since P is M♮-convex, its first k layers are also M♮-convex after plank intersection. Define c_{k+1} using the displayed minimum, which is finite because P is bounded and the relevant exchange supports are nonempty. The M-convex exchange condition within a single layer and the already-known M♮ inequalities for h_k handle all pairs not involving the new top layer. For x in the new top layer and y in a lower layer, the choice of c_{k+1} forces h_{k+1}(x)+h_{k+1}(y)≥h_{k+1}(x-e_j)+h_{k+1}(y+e_j) for a suitable j, exactly the missing M♮ exchange inequality. Thus h_{k+1} is M♮-convex.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 5.12
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let f,g be sparse paving valuated matroids. This means f and g are valuated matroids and their minimizer matroids argmin(f) and argmin(g) are sparse paving matroids. If f ↠_min g, then there exists an M♮-convex function h:{0,1}^E→ℝ∪{∞} with h↑=f and h↓=g. Hence, for sparse paving valuated matroids, the conditions in Theorem 1.3 are equivalent.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Let M=argmin(f), N=argmin(g), and let c_f,c_g be the minimum values of f and g. Define h to be f on the top rank, g on the bottom rank, the constant min(c_f,c_g) on all intermediate hypercube layers, and ∞ outside these layers. Each layer is M-convex. To verify the M♮ exchange inequality, suppose an inequality failed for x,y. If x is not in the top layer, then failure for two distinct exchange coordinates would force two nonbases of N whose coordinatewise minimum has rank greater than rk(N)-2, contradicting sparse paving; the bottom/top symmetric case is analogous for M. The one-coordinate case is tautological. Thus h is M♮-convex.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 5.13, Proposition 5.15, and Theorem 5.16
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For M-convex functions f_0,...,f_k with finite support and any u∈(ℝ^E)^*, the minimizer of their convolution satisfies (f_0□...□f_k)^u=f_0^u+...+f_k^u, where □ denotes infimal convolution. If f,g,h have support in the nonnegative orthant and g ↠_min f and h ↠_min f, then g□h ↠_min f. More generally, if f_0,...,f_k are M-convex functions with f_{i+1} ↠_min f_i for all i, then for every u the minimizer (f_0□...□f_k)^u is a flag M-convex set of the rank type determined by rk(f_0),...,rk(f_k), up to a common lift shift and coordinate-summing projection.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The minimizer formula follows from the fact that exposed faces of a Minkowski sum are sums of exposed faces; applying this to the convex hulls of epigraphs gives minimizers of infimal convolution. For Proposition 5.15, fix u. Then (g□h)^u=g^u+h^u. Because supports lie in the nonnegative orthant, the submodular function of h^u is nondecreasing; adding such an M-convex set to g^u preserves the quotient relation to f^u by Lemma 5.14. For Theorem 5.16, apply the minimizer formula to get a sum of a flag of M-convex sets f_i^u. The finite-set compression criterion, Proposition 4.14, then identifies this sum as a flag M-convex set.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 6.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let P,Q⊆ℝ^E be generalized permutohedra with corresponding real-valued submodular functions p,q. The following are equivalent, giving the nonintegral analogue of an M-convex quotient. (1) q(Y)-q(X)≤p(Y)-p(X) for all X⊆Y⊆E. (2) For every permutation σ, the greedy vertices x_σ∈P and y_σ∈Q satisfy x_σ≥y_σ. (3) For every X⊆E, S(q/X)⊆S(p/X). (4) G(p,q#) is a generalized polymatroid with top face P and bottom face Q. (5) There exists a generalized permutohedron R⊆ℝ^{E⊔X}, with X chosen as a singleton if desired, such that P=R∖X and Q=R/X. (6) For all x∈Q, y∈P, and i∈supp+(x-y), there exist j∈supp−(x-y) and ρ_0>0 such that x-ρ(e_i-e_j)∈Q and y+ρ(e_i-e_j)∈P for all ρ∈[0,ρ_0]. (7) The polyhedron (B(p)+ℝ^E_{≤0})∩(B(q)+ℝ^E_{≥0}) has only edge directions e_i-e_j and e_i. (8) There exists a generalized permutohedron Γ⊆ℝ^E×ℝ^{Ē} and a generalized permutohedron W⊆ℝ^{Ē} such that P is the top layer of the left projection of Γ and Q=ind_Γ(W). (9) There are linking permutohedra Γ,Δ⊆ℝ^E×ℝ^E with P,Q as top layers of their left sets and Γ⪯_RΔ in Green's right preorder on the monoid of linking permutohedra.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper states that the proofs are parallel to the integral case. The vertex criterion uses the same greedy-coordinate formula, now with real values. The contraction/submodular-polyhedron containment equivalence is algebraic. The top-bottom and deletion-contraction equivalences use the continuous generalized-polymatroid theory: generalized polymatroids are precisely projections of generalized permutohedra, and their top and bottom faces are the base polyhedra B(p) and B(q). The exchange condition is replaced by Murota's continuous exchange property for base polyhedra, allowing motion by a small real amount ρ in direction e_i-e_j. The edge-direction criterion characterizes generalized polymatroids by allowable edge directions. Induction and Green-preorder arguments repeat the linking-set proofs with generalized permutohedra and possibly unbounded linking permutohedra.

proof_techniques.name: Encode quotients as top and bottom layers
proof_techniques.description: The paper repeatedly converts a quotient P ↠ Q into a single M♮-convex object R whose top layer is P and bottom layer is Q. Polyhedrally, R is the generalized polymatroid G(p,q#), the intersection of the submodular polyhedron above Q and below P. This packages all quotient inequalities into one convex-geometric object and lets one use layer operations, plank intersections, and projection/lifting.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If elements of the relative Dressian are represented by valuated matroid quotients, a candidate involution might be studied by asking how it acts on the associated top-bottom object. For the flat subposet, this technique suggests identifying F↦F^⊥ with a layer-reversal or dual-supermodular operation on the corresponding generalized polymatroid.

proof_techniques.name: Use minimizers to test valuated quotient relations
proof_techniques.description: For M-convex functions, especially valuated matroids, quotient conditions can be checked on all linear minimizer sets. The paper proves that the exchange quotient condition implies every pair of minimizers is an M-convex quotient, and for valuated matroids it records the converse via Brandt-Eur-Zhang. Thus a valuated quotient is controlled by the matroid quotients appearing in every cell of its regular subdivision.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Tropical linear spaces are represented by valuated matroids, so an order-reversing operation on a relative Dressian could be tested cellwise on all initial matroids. This may reduce extension of F↦F^⊥ to verifying compatibility with minimizer matroid quotients under all weights.

proof_techniques.name: Greedy vertex inequalities
proof_techniques.description: For M-convex sets, the quotient inequality q(Y)-q(X)≤p(Y)-p(X) is equivalent to coordinatewise comparison x_σ≥y_σ of all greedy vertices indexed by permutations. This transforms a universal family of subset inequalities into a finite family of vertex comparisons along maximal chains.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If a relative Dressian element or its underlying matroid quotient can be described by rank functions, an attempted involution can be checked against chain/flag data rather than all subsets. It may also give finite obstructions to the existence of a global order-reversing map extending the flat involution.

proof_techniques.name: Induction through linking sets and linking functions
proof_techniques.description: The paper generalizes induction by bipartite graphs to M-convex sets and M-convex functions. A linking set Γ⊆ℤ^V×ℤ^U or linking function γ acts on an M-convex set/function W by selecting x for which (x,-w) lies in Γ, or by infimizing γ(x,-w)+r(w). Quotients are exactly those objects obtainable as the top left layer of a linking set together with an induction output.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This gives a way to model morphisms or inclusions of tropical linear spaces as combinatorial/tropical linear maps. A putative duality on Dr(M) might be constructed by transposing linking data, while failure of such a transposition to preserve the required Green preorder could obstruct an involution.

proof_techniques.name: Green preorder of the linking-set monoid
proof_techniques.description: Linking sets from a ground set to itself form a monoid under relational product. The paper identifies quotient relations with Green's right preorder on this monoid: Γ⪯_RΔ forces the top left set of Γ to quotient the top left set of Δ, and every quotient arises in this way. This imports semigroup order tools into quotient theory.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: An order-reversing involution on a relative Dressian might correspond to an anti-automorphism of an appropriate linking monoid, such as transposition turning right preorder into left preorder. The paper also notes the monoid is not regular for |V|>2, signaling that naive inverse-based constructions may not be available.

proof_techniques.name: Compatible matroid lifts
proof_techniques.description: The paper constructs matroid lifts of arbitrary bounded M-convex sets by splitting each coordinate into unit intervals and using a coordinate-summing projection. Quotients are preserved under compatible box lifts, and if lifted matroids form quotients then their projections form quotients. This allows general M-convex quotient questions to be reduced to classical matroid quotients when the lifts are chosen compatibly.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Since the original problem starts with an ordinary matroid M but Dr(M) involves valuated/tropical objects, compatible lifts offer a route for reducing statements about generalized quotient data to classical matroid quotient and flat-lattice statements.

proof_techniques.name: Flag polytope compression
proof_techniques.description: A pair P ↠ Q can be encoded by the Minkowski sum P+Q: after compatible matroid lifting, this sum is a projection of a flag matroid polytope, equivalently a flag M-convex set. For M-convex functions, convolution has minimizers equal to sums of minimizers, so valuated quotient flags yield subdivisions whose cells are flag M-convex sets.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Relative Dressian elements are naturally polyhedral objects. This technique suggests looking for an order-reversing operation as a duality on subdivisions of flag polytopes, or showing nonexistence by detecting that no such subdivision duality can extend the prescribed flat duality.

proof_techniques.name: Canonical quotients from minors and truncations
proof_techniques.description: Projection of an M-convex set to a subset of coordinates is M♮-convex, and its layers are basic minors. Higher layers quotient lower layers. Truncation is the maximal quotient of a fixed rank drop: every quotient of that rank is contained in the corresponding truncation.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The natural inclusion of the opposite flat lattice into a relative Dressian via contractions resembles the paper's quotient-from-minor construction. Truncation maximality may provide canonical bounds on where an order-reversing extension could send elements.

proof_techniques.name: Layer-shifting for flags of M-convex functions
proof_techniques.description: Pairwise elementary exchange quotients f_i ↠_Ex f_{i-1} do not automatically assemble into one M♮-convex function, because relative additive constants between layers matter. Theorem 1.4 shows that if the union of supports is M♮-convex, constants can be chosen recursively so that the shifted functions become the layers of an M♮-convex function.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If constructing Φ requires extending behavior from flats or elementary quotients to longer chains in Dr(M), this theorem suggests that layer constants or valuations may be adjustable, but only under a support-level M♮-convexity condition.

proof_techniques.name: Continuous generalized-permutohedron relaxation
proof_techniques.description: The final section drops integrality and proves the same quotient characterizations for generalized permutohedra, replacing discrete exchange by a local continuous exchange direction. The quotient cone becomes a deformation cone of a projected permutohedron.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the relative Dressian is studied as a fan or polyhedral moduli space, the continuous relaxation may reveal linear or cone-theoretic obstructions to an order-reversing involution before imposing integrality or valuated-matroid realizability.

other_useful_info: The paper explicitly cites Haque 2012 and Brandt-Eur-Zhang 2021 for quotients of valuated matroids and tropical flag varieties; Remark 5.7 uses Brandt-Eur-Zhang's theorem equating valuated quotient exchange with flag-matroid subdivision data. Remark 5.8 notes that a deletion-contraction factorization for quotient-like morphisms is subtle beyond ordinary matroids and is not fully settled for valuated matroids; it points to Jarra-Lorscheid 2024 for matroids with coefficients. Remark 2.43 relates top-bottom quotient data to delta-matroids and jump systems, with saturated delta-matroids corresponding to generalized matroids; this may be relevant if an involution naturally acts on nonconsecutive layers. Question 4.16 asks for a more direct intrinsic description of flag M-convex polytopes, indicating that the current compression characterization depends on projections of flag matroid polytopes. Remark 6.2 observes that the space of quotients of generalized permutohedra is the deformation cone of an orthogonal projection of the regular permutohedron; M-convex quotients are its integer points and matroid quotients its 0/1 points.
why_relevant: Unifies quotients of matroids, valuated matroids, polymatroids, and M-convex functions. Since Dr(M) is naturally a quotient poset of valuated matroids, its equivalent quotient criteria are likely useful for constructing or obstructing an order-reversing involution.
